questions.questionText: An organism that must consume other organisms for energy is called a(n):

answerOptions.text: Producer
answerOptions.rationale: A producer (like a plant) makes its own food.

answerOptions.text: Autotroph
answerOptions.rationale: An autotroph is another name for a producer.

answerOptions.text: Consumer (or Heterotroph)
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Consumers obtain energy by feeding on other organisms.

answerOptions.text: Decomposer
answerOptions.rationale: A decomposer breaks down dead organic matter.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what was a significant conclusion drawn from the discovery of corpuscles regarding the nature of atoms?

answerOptions.text: Atoms are now understood to be much lighter than the corpuscles they contain.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states corpuscles are 'much smaller than atoms' and have a 'far smaller' mass-to-charge ratio than the lightest atom, implying corpuscles are lighter than atoms, not the other way around.

answerOptions.text: The long-held belief that atoms are indivisible units of matter was disproven.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage explicitly states, 'This discovery shows that the atom is not indivisible, as once supposed, but contains constituent parts.'

answerOptions.text: The differences between substances are due to the unique types of corpuscles each atom possesses.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states, 'the differences between substances may be due to the arrangement of particles which are themselves identical,' indicating corpuscles are identical, not unique for each atom.

answerOptions.text: Corpuscles are the fundamental units of electricity, but not constituents of atoms.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states 'Electricity itself appears to be carried by these corpuscles' AND that atoms 'contains constituent parts' which are corpuscles. So they are constituents of atoms.

questions.passage:
Cathode Rays and the Corpuscle (Excerpt)

J. J. Thomson, 1897

In studying the discharge of electricity through gases at low pressure, I observed that the rays coming from the cathode could be deflected by electric and magnetic fields. From these deflections I calculated the ratio of their mass to their charge and found it to be far smaller than that of the lightest atom known. The only reasonable inference was that these rays consist of particles, which I have called corpuscles, much smaller than atoms, and common to all kinds of matter. This discovery shows that the atom is not indivisible, as once supposed, but contains constituent parts. Electricity itself appears to be carried by these corpuscles. We are thus admitted, as it were, into the inner structure of matter, and we see that the differences between substances may be due to the arrangement of particles which are themselves identical.

questions.questionText: What specific evidence, as described in the passage, led the researcher to infer that corpuscles are 'much smaller than atoms'?

answerOptions.text: The ability of cathode rays to be deflected by electric and magnetic fields.
answerOptions.rationale: The deflection by fields was an observation that allowed for calculations, but it was the *result* of those calculations that indicated their size relative to atoms.

answerOptions.text: The finding that corpuscles are common to all kinds of matter.
answerOptions.rationale: While corpuscles being common to all matter is a conclusion, it is not the evidence used to determine they are smaller than atoms.

answerOptions.text: The calculation showing their mass-to-charge ratio was significantly less than that of the lightest atom.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states, 'From these deflections I calculated the ratio of their mass to their charge and found it to be far smaller than that of the lightest atom known. The only reasonable inference was that these rays consist of particles... much smaller than atoms.' This directly links the calculation to the inference about size.

answerOptions.text: The observation that electricity itself appears to be carried by these corpuscles.
answerOptions.rationale: This is another conclusion about the nature of corpuscles, not the evidence used to determine their size relative to atoms.

questions.passage:
Cathode Rays and the Corpuscle (Excerpt)

J. J. Thomson, 1897

In studying the discharge of electricity through gases at low pressure, I observed that the rays coming from the cathode could be deflected by electric and magnetic fields. From these deflections I calculated the ratio of their mass to their charge and found it to be far smaller than that of the lightest atom known. The only reasonable inference was that these rays consist of particles, which I have called corpuscles, much smaller than atoms, and common to all kinds of matter. This discovery shows that the atom is not indivisible, as once supposed, but contains constituent parts. Electricity itself appears to be carried by these corpuscles. We are thus admitted, as it were, into the inner structure of matter, and we see that the differences between substances may be due to the arrangement of particles which are themselves identical.

questions.questionText:
A student measures the mass and volume of four different objects. The results are shown in the table below.
ObjectMass (g)Volume (cm³)
Rock A15060
Wood Block B80100
Metal Cylinder C27030
Plastic Sphere D4550
Based on the data, which object has the greatest density?

answerOptions.text: Rock A
answerOptions.rationale: To find the density of Rock A, divide its mass by its volume: 150 g / 60 cm³ = 2.5 g/cm³. This is not the greatest density among the options.

answerOptions.text: Wood Block B
answerOptions.rationale: To find the density of Wood Block B, divide its mass by its volume: 80 g / 100 cm³ = 0.8 g/cm³. This is not the greatest density among the options.

answerOptions.text: Metal Cylinder C
answerOptions.rationale: To find the density of each object, divide its mass by its volume. For Metal Cylinder C: 270 g / 30 cm³ = 9.0 g/cm³. Comparing this to Rock A (2.5 g/cm³), Wood Block B (0.8 g/cm³), and Plastic Sphere D (0.9 g/cm³), Metal Cylinder C has the greatest density.

answerOptions.text: Plastic Sphere D
answerOptions.rationale: To find the density of Plastic Sphere D, divide its mass by its volume: 45 g / 50 cm³ = 0.9 g/cm³. This is not the greatest density among the options.

questions.questionText: In the element table, lithium has an atomic mass of 7 and 3 protons. What value should replace X for the number of neutrons in lithium?

answerOptions.rationale: Three is the number of protons, not the number of neutrons.
Correct. Neutrons equal atomic mass minus protons, so 7 − 3 = 4.
Seven is the atomic mass, not the neutron count.
This does not match the relationship shown in the table.

questions.questionText: According to the carbon-cycle diagram, which process removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and stores it in organic matter?

answerOptions.text: Photosynthesis
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Photosynthesis takes in atmospheric carbon dioxide and converts it into organic carbon in plants.

answerOptions.text: Auto and factory emissions
answerOptions.rationale: Those emissions add carbon dioxide to the atmosphere rather than removing it.

answerOptions.text: Animal respiration
answerOptions.rationale: Respiration releases carbon dioxide back to the atmosphere.

answerOptions.text: Combustion of fossil fuels
answerOptions.rationale: Burning fossil fuels adds carbon dioxide to the air.

stimulusImage.alt: A grayscale diagram illustrating the interconnected processes of the carbon cycle on Earth.

questions.questionText: What is the author's primary argument regarding the most critical requirement in a hospital?

answerOptions.text: Hospitals must prioritize specialized medical treatments for complex diseases.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage emphasizes environmental factors as the 'very first requirement' to do the sick no harm, not specialized treatments.

answerOptions.text: The initial disease a patient suffers from is the sole determinant of their survival.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage explicitly states 'many patients perish not from their original disease but from foul air, bad drainage, and want of cleanliness,' contradicting this option.

answerOptions.text: Ensuring a clean, well-ventilated, and properly drained environment is fundamental to preventing patient harm and promoting recovery.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage highlights 'foul air, bad drainage, and want of cleanliness' as causes of death and lists 'pure air, pure water, efficient drainage, cleanliness, and light' as essential for the patient's best condition.

answerOptions.text: The main duty of a nurse is to comfort patients and manage their pain.
answerOptions.rationale: While comfort is part of nursing, the passage specifically defines the nurse's duty as placing the patient in 'the best possible condition for nature to act upon him,' focusing on environmental management.

questions.passage:
Notes on Nursing (Excerpt)

Florence Nightingale, 1859

The very first requirement in a hospital is that it should do the sick no harm. Yet many patients perish not from their original disease but from foul air, bad drainage, and want of cleanliness. The nurse’s duty is to place the patient in the best possible condition for nature to act upon him. This means pure air, pure water, efficient drainage, cleanliness, and light. The sick should not breathe the air they have just exhaled. Windows must be opened, beds aired, and rooms kept free of needless noise. What is called trained nursing is the knowledge of how to manage the patient’s environment. It is no small thing, for on it often depends whether life or death shall prevail.

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, why is 'managing the patient's environment' considered a crucial aspect of 'trained nursing'?

answerOptions.text: It is a simple task that requires minimal training and skill.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states, 'It is no small thing, for on it often depends whether life or death shall prevail,' indicating its significance and complexity.

answerOptions.text: It ensures that nurses can effectively administer complex medications and surgical procedures.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage defines managing the environment in terms of pure air, water, drainage, cleanliness, and light, not medication or surgery administration.

answerOptions.text: It directly influences a patient's chances of survival and recovery by preventing harm from unsanitary conditions.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage links environmental factors like foul air and bad drainage to patients perishing and states that managing the environment often determines 'whether life or death shall prevail.'

answerOptions.text: It allows nurses to maintain silence in hospitals, which is the most important factor for healing.
answerOptions.rationale: While 'rooms kept free of needless noise' is mentioned, it is one item among many environmental factors, and not described as the *most* important or the sole reason for environmental management's cruciality.

questions.passage:
Notes on Nursing (Excerpt)

Florence Nightingale, 1859

The very first requirement in a hospital is that it should do the sick no harm. Yet many patients perish not from their original disease but from foul air, bad drainage, and want of cleanliness. The nurse’s duty is to place the patient in the best possible condition for nature to act upon him. This means pure air, pure water, efficient drainage, cleanliness, and light. The sick should not breathe the air they have just exhaled. Windows must be opened, beds aired, and rooms kept free of needless noise. What is called trained nursing is the knowledge of how to manage the patient’s environment. It is no small thing, for on it often depends whether life or death shall prevail.

questions.questionText:
A scientist investigated how different light intensities affect the growth of a specific plant species over a 20-day period. The results are summarized in the table below.
Light Intensity (lux)Initial Height (cm)Final Height (cm)Growth Period (days)
100010.012.520
500010.018.020
1000010.015.020
Based on the data, what was the average daily growth rate for the plants exposed to a light intensity of 5000 lux?

answerOptions.text: 0.125 cm/day
answerOptions.rationale: This is the daily growth rate for plants exposed to 1000 lux (2.5 cm total growth / 20 days).

answerOptions.text: 0.400 cm/day
answerOptions.rationale: For plants under 5000 lux, the total growth was 18.0 cm - 10.0 cm = 8.0 cm. The average daily growth rate is 8.0 cm / 20 days = 0.400 cm/day.

answerOptions.text: 0.250 cm/day
answerOptions.rationale: This is the daily growth rate for plants exposed to 10000 lux (5.0 cm total growth / 20 days).

answerOptions.text: 8.0 cm/day
answerOptions.rationale: This is the total growth (8.0 cm) for the plants under 5000 lux, not the average daily growth rate.

questions.questionText: According to the 2012 energy-consumption pie chart, what percentage of U.S. energy came from fossil fuels?

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Petroleum (36%), natural gas (27%), and coal (18%) add to 81%.
That is only the share from petroleum.
That total is too low for the combined fossil-fuel share.

questions.questionText: Why does the figure include both a main pie chart and a breakdown of renewable energy sources?

answerOptions.text: To show both renewable energy’s share of total use and the different sources within that category.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The main pie chart shows renewable energy as one category, and the expanded graphic shows what makes up that category.

answerOptions.text: To prove renewable energy was larger than all fossil fuels combined.
answerOptions.rationale: The chart shows the opposite; fossil fuels make up the much larger share.

answerOptions.text: To show that nuclear power is a form of renewable energy.
answerOptions.rationale: Nuclear electric power is shown as a separate category.

answerOptions.text: To replace the need for any percentages in the graphic.
answerOptions.rationale: The figure still relies on percentages and totals for interpretation.

questions.questionText:
Plant SampleLight Intensity (lux)Water Volume (mL/day)Weekly Growth (cm)
Plant A5001002.5
Plant B10001004.0
Plant C5001503.0
Plant D10001505.5
Based on the data, what is the average increase in weekly growth when light intensity is increased from 500 lux to 1000 lux, assuming water volume is kept constant?

answerOptions.text: 1.0 cm
answerOptions.rationale: This is incorrect. The differences in growth for constant water volumes are 1.5 cm (4.0 - 2.5) and 2.5 cm (5.5 - 3.0), so the average is (1.5 + 2.5) / 2 = 2.0 cm.

answerOptions.text: 1.5 cm
answerOptions.rationale: This is incorrect. While 1.5 cm is the increase observed when water volume is 100 mL/day, it does not represent the average increase across both tested water volumes.

answerOptions.text: 2.0 cm
answerOptions.rationale: To determine the average increase: For plants receiving 100 mL/day of water, growth increased by 4.0 cm - 2.5 cm = 1.5 cm. For plants receiving 150 mL/day of water, growth increased by 5.5 cm - 3.0 cm = 2.5 cm. The average increase is (1.5 cm + 2.5 cm) / 2 = 4.0 cm / 2 = 2.0 cm.

answerOptions.text: 2.5 cm
answerOptions.rationale: This is incorrect. While 2.5 cm is the increase observed when water volume is 150 mL/day, it does not represent the average increase across both tested water volumes.

Temperature (°C)Amylase Activity (units/min)
2015

Based on the data, what is the approximate percentage decrease in amylase activity when the temperature increases from 40°C to 50°C?

answerOptions.rationale: The amylase activity at 40°C is 80 units/min, and at 50°C it is 60 units/min. The decrease is 80 - 60 = 20 units/min. The percentage decrease is (20 / 80) * 100 = 25%.

The amylase activity at 40°C is 80 units/min, and at 50°C it is 60 units/min. The decrease is 80 - 60 = 20 units/min. To find the percentage decrease, divide the decrease by the original activity: (20 / 80) * 100 = 25%.

The amylase activity at 40°C is 80 units/min, and at 50°C it is 60 units/min. The decrease is 80 - 60 = 20 units/min. The percentage decrease is (20 / 80) * 100 = 25%. This option might result from an incorrect calculation, such as dividing by the final activity instead of the original.

questions.questionText: Moist air moves inland from the ocean in the direction of the prevailing winds. Based on the diagram, which labeled location would most likely receive the least precipitation?

answerOptions.text: Point A
answerOptions.rationale: Point A is beside the ocean on the windward side, where moist air first arrives.

answerOptions.text: Point B
answerOptions.rationale: Point B is on the rising side of the mountain, where cooling air is more likely to condense.

answerOptions.text: Point C
answerOptions.rationale: Point C is near the mountain crest, not in the driest rain-shadow location.

answerOptions.text: Point E
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Point E is on the leeward side, where descending air is warmer and drier.

stimulusImage.alt: Diagram showing prevailing winds causing a rain shadow effect over mountains near an ocean, with elevation and distance scales.

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, how does the author explain the development of a species with features like longer necks or webbed feet over time?

answerOptions.text: Species are formed instantly with all their features already present.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states, "No species has been formed all at once. Nature never makes leaps."

answerOptions.text: Specific needs and habits cause organs to change, and these changes are inherited by offspring.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage explains that "the frequent use of any organ strengthens and develops it... and second, that these acquired developments are transmitted to offspring."

answerOptions.text: Simpler life forms spontaneously generate more complex organs directly.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage mentions spontaneous generation for the earliest, simplest forms, but not as the mechanism for developing complex organs within existing species. Organ complexity is developed through use/disuse and inheritance.

answerOptions.text: Environmental factors lead to random, disconnected changes in individual organisms.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage describes a guided process where 'environment, need, and habit act together' to cause changes, and these changes are passed on, not random and disconnected.

questions.passage:
Zoological Philosophy (Excerpt)

Jean-Baptiste Lamarck, 1809

No species has been formed all at once. Nature never makes leaps. From the simplest forms of life, produced by spontaneous generation in the earliest ages, there has been a slow and continual progress toward more complex organization. This progress is guided by two great laws: first, that the frequent use of any organ strengthens and develops it, while the disuse of it weakens and diminishes it; and second, that these acquired developments are transmitted to offspring. Thus, in a race of animals that has long been obliged to wade in water, the toes may spread and a membrane may form; in others, the neck may lengthen to reach leaves. Environment, need, and habit act together. Though later naturalists may improve upon this theory, I submit that it explains the great variety of living forms by natural causes rather than by separate acts of creation.

questions.questionText: The author states that his theory "explains the great variety of living forms by natural causes rather than by separate acts of creation." What does this statement reveal about the author's primary goal?

answerOptions.text: To propose that all species were once identical and have since diversified rapidly.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage describes a 'slow and continual progress' from simpler to more complex forms, not necessarily starting from identical forms that diversified rapidly.

answerOptions.text: To challenge the idea that species were independently designed and created.
answerOptions.rationale: By contrasting 'natural causes' with 'separate acts of creation,' the author explicitly aims to provide an alternative, natural explanation for the diversity of life, thereby challenging creationist views.

answerOptions.text: To argue that only the simplest life forms are subject to natural processes of change.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage discusses a 'slow and continual progress toward more complex organization' for the variety of living forms, not just the simplest.

answerOptions.text: To emphasize that future scientific theories will entirely replace current evolutionary ideas.
answerOptions.rationale: The author acknowledges that 'later naturalists may improve upon this theory,' but does not suggest it will be entirely replaced or discredited.

questions.passage:
Zoological Philosophy (Excerpt)

Jean-Baptiste Lamarck, 1809

No species has been formed all at once. Nature never makes leaps. From the simplest forms of life, produced by spontaneous generation in the earliest ages, there has been a slow and continual progress toward more complex organization. This progress is guided by two great laws: first, that the frequent use of any organ strengthens and develops it, while the disuse of it weakens and diminishes it; and second, that these acquired developments are transmitted to offspring. Thus, in a race of animals that has long been obliged to wade in water, the toes may spread and a membrane may form; in others, the neck may lengthen to reach leaves. Environment, need, and habit act together. Though later naturalists may improve upon this theory, I submit that it explains the great variety of living forms by natural causes rather than by separate acts of creation.

questions.questionText: What conclusion is best supported by the hurricane image and the sea-surface temperature data shown with it?

answerOptions.text: Warmer ocean water can provide energy that helps a hurricane strengthen.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The paired image and data are meant to connect hurricane strength with warm ocean water as an energy source.

answerOptions.text: Hurricanes form only when ocean water is below freezing.
answerOptions.rationale: The figure links hurricanes with warm water, not freezing conditions.

answerOptions.text: Ocean temperature has no effect on storm intensity.
answerOptions.rationale: The purpose of the table is to show that water temperature is relevant to storm development.

answerOptions.text: Hurricanes get all of their energy from land surfaces.
answerOptions.rationale: The image focuses on an ocean-based storm and water-temperature data, not land heating.

stimulusImage.alt: Satellite image of a hurricane from space with a table listing average temperatures of large bodies of water.

questions.questionText: Why is the temperature table useful alongside the satellite view of the storm?

answerOptions.text: It gives numerical evidence that can be used to explain the storm conditions shown in the image.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The table adds measured data that helps explain what is seen in the satellite image.

answerOptions.text: It identifies the names of every city in the storm path.
answerOptions.rationale: A temperature table provides environmental measurements, not city labels.

answerOptions.text: It proves the storm has already ended.
answerOptions.rationale: Temperature data can support analysis of storm conditions, but it does not by itself prove the storm has ended.

answerOptions.text: It replaces the need to interpret the satellite image at all.
answerOptions.rationale: The two sources are strongest when interpreted together rather than used as substitutes for each other.

stimulusImage.alt: Satellite image of a hurricane from space with a table listing average temperatures of large bodies of water.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what is a key advantage of alternating current (AC) over direct current (DC) for transmitting electric power?

answerOptions.text: AC systems can generate electricity more cheaply than DC systems.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage does not discuss the cost of generation, but rather the efficiency of transmission.

answerOptions.text: AC allows for efficient voltage transformation, making long-distance transmission practical.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states, 'Alternating current... can be transformed to higher voltages, transmitted over long lines, and then reduced again for practical use,' highlighting this as a key benefit over DC's losses over distance.

answerOptions.text: DC systems are unable to power homes and machinery effectively.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage implies DC suffers losses over distance, but does not state it is unable to power homes or machinery at all, only that AC will make electricity a 'universal servant'.

answerOptions.text: AC produces less heat during transmission than DC.
answerOptions.rationale: While 'great losses' in DC systems might imply more heat, the passage specifically points to voltage transformation as the primary advantage of AC, not explicitly less heat production.

questions.passage:
On the Use of Alternating Currents (Excerpt)

Nikola Tesla, 1892

In considering the transmission of electric power, it became evident to me that direct current systems would always suffer great losses over distance. Alternating current, on the other hand, can be transformed to higher voltages, transmitted over long lines, and then reduced again for practical use. By employing rotating magnetic fields and polyphase systems, we can construct motors that are both efficient and reliable, with few parts to wear. The spread of such systems will make it possible to harness waterfalls and distant sources of energy for the service of cities. Electricity, so managed, will become not a curiosity but a universal servant, lighting homes, driving machinery, and improving daily life.

questions.questionText: Based on the author's vision, what primary societal transformation is expected from the widespread adoption of alternating current systems?

answerOptions.text: Electricity will become a scientific curiosity, inspiring further research.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states electricity will become 'not a curiosity but a universal servant,' which contradicts this option.

answerOptions.text: Urban areas will experience fewer power outages due to the reliability of AC motors.
answerOptions.rationale: While AC motors are mentioned as reliable, the passage focuses on a broader societal transformation of electricity's role, not specifically on reducing power outages as the primary outcome.

answerOptions.text: Electrical power will transition from a limited resource to a ubiquitous service for everyday life.
answerOptions.rationale: The author envisions electricity becoming 'not a curiosity but a universal servant, lighting homes, driving machinery, and improving daily life,' indicating a transformation into a widely available and essential service.

answerOptions.text: Waterfalls and distant energy sources will become the only viable means of generating electricity.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states that these systems will 'make it possible to harness waterfalls and distant sources,' but does not suggest they will become the exclusive means of generation.

questions.passage:
On the Use of Alternating Currents (Excerpt)

Nikola Tesla, 1892

In considering the transmission of electric power, it became evident to me that direct current systems would always suffer great losses over distance. Alternating current, on the other hand, can be transformed to higher voltages, transmitted over long lines, and then reduced again for practical use. By employing rotating magnetic fields and polyphase systems, we can construct motors that are both efficient and reliable, with few parts to wear. The spread of such systems will make it possible to harness waterfalls and distant sources of energy for the service of cities. Electricity, so managed, will become not a curiosity but a universal servant, lighting homes, driving machinery, and improving daily life.

questions.questionText: What variable is plotted on the y-axis of the relative humidity graph?

answerOptions.text: Humidity as a percentage
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The vertical axis is labeled with humidity percentages.

answerOptions.text: Air temperature in degrees Celsius
answerOptions.rationale: The graph is specifically labeled as relative humidity, not temperature.

answerOptions.text: Wind speed in kilometers per hour
answerOptions.rationale: Wind speed is not the variable shown on the y-axis.

answerOptions.text: Rainfall in centimeters
answerOptions.rationale: The graph does not plot precipitation totals.

stimulusImage.alt: Line graph showing relative humidity percentage over eight days, from May 1 to May 8.

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, what can be inferred about the relationship between different natural elements in the Sierra Nevada?

answerOptions.text: Each element functions independently, contributing to overall health.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states, 'When we try to pick out anything by itself, we find it hitched to everything else in the universe,' directly contradicting the idea of independent functioning.

answerOptions.text: The destruction of one element often has isolated, limited effects.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage warns, 'To destroy the forests is to dry the fountains that feed the valleys,' indicating far-reaching and interconnected consequences, not isolated effects.

answerOptions.text: Natural elements are interdependent, forming a cohesive and balanced system.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage emphasizes that elements are 'hitched to everything else,' 'bound together in one harmonious whole,' and 'animals in their turn scatter seeds and keep the balance,' all highlighting interdependence and a cohesive system.

answerOptions.text: Human intervention is required to maintain the harmony among natural elements.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage describes natural processes like animals scattering seeds that 'keep the balance,' suggesting nature maintains its own harmony, with human actions often being destructive rather than beneficial for balance.

questions.passage:
The Mountains of California (Excerpt)

John Muir, 1894

When we try to pick out anything by itself, we find it hitched to everything else in the universe. Nowhere is this more visible than in the Sierra Nevada, where glacier-meadows, forests, and streams are bound together in one harmonious whole. The pines draw their life from the soil made by ancient ice; the waterfalls feed the ferns and lilies; the animals in their turn scatter seeds and keep the balance. To destroy the forests is to dry the fountains that feed the valleys. Nature’s beauty is not mere decoration, but a sign of health. I would have people go to the mountains not only for recreation but for instruction, to learn that man is only one of the Creator’s creatures and must live in sympathy with all the rest.

questions.questionText: The author suggests that 'Nature's beauty is not mere decoration, but a sign of health.' What is the most logical interpretation of this statement within the context of the passage?

answerOptions.text: Beautiful natural landscapes are primarily valuable for recreational activities.
answerOptions.rationale: While the author mentions recreation, the statement connects beauty to 'health,' implying a deeper ecological significance beyond just recreational value.

answerOptions.text: The aesthetic appeal of nature directly reflects its ecological stability and well-being.
answerOptions.rationale: By describing 'glacier-meadows, forests, and streams' as 'bound together in one harmonious whole' and then stating 'Nature's beauty is... a sign of health,' the author links visual beauty to the overall ecological balance and vitality of the environment.

answerOptions.text: Human appreciation for nature's beauty is essential for its preservation.
answerOptions.rationale: The statement focuses on beauty as an indicator of health, not on human appreciation as a prerequisite for that health or preservation. The author's purpose is for people to learn from nature, not for their appreciation to be the primary factor for its health.

answerOptions.text: Natural beauty is a fragile quality that can easily be lost without human intervention.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage implies that human destruction, such as destroying forests, harms nature's health and beauty, rather than suggesting that human intervention is needed to prevent its loss.

questions.passage:
The Mountains of California (Excerpt)

John Muir, 1894

When we try to pick out anything by itself, we find it hitched to everything else in the universe. Nowhere is this more visible than in the Sierra Nevada, where glacier-meadows, forests, and streams are bound together in one harmonious whole. The pines draw their life from the soil made by ancient ice; the waterfalls feed the ferns and lilies; the animals in their turn scatter seeds and keep the balance. To destroy the forests is to dry the fountains that feed the valleys. Nature’s beauty is not mere decoration, but a sign of health. I would have people go to the mountains not only for recreation but for instruction, to learn that man is only one of the Creator’s creatures and must live in sympathy with all the rest.

questions.questionText: Based on the completed Punnett square, what is the genotype ratio of the offspring?

answerOptions.text: 1 GG : 2 Gg : 1 gg
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The four outcomes are one GG, two Gg, and one gg.

answerOptions.text: 3 GG : 1 gg
answerOptions.rationale: The completed square includes two heterozygous outcomes, not three GG outcomes.

answerOptions.text: 2 GG : 1 Gg : 1 gg
answerOptions.rationale: Only one GG square appears in the passage.

answerOptions.text: 1 GG : 1 Gg : 2 gg
answerOptions.rationale: Only one gg square appears in the completed square.

questions.passage:
A biology class completes a Punnett square for two heterozygous flower plants, Gg x Gg. In this trait, G is the dominant allele for purple flowers and g is the recessive allele for white flowers.

Gg
GGGGg
gGggg

Use the completed Punnett square to answer the questions.

questions.questionText: What is the main conclusion the author draws from observing planets revolving about Jupiter?

answerOptions.text: The Earth is not the center of all celestial orbits.
answerOptions.rationale: The author explicitly states that these observations prove 'not all heavenly bodies revolve around the Earth,' directly challenging the former geocentric belief.

answerOptions.text: Jupiter has a stronger gravitational pull than Earth.
answerOptions.rationale: While possibly true, the passage focuses on the orbital mechanics relative to Earth and the implications for the existing model, not the comparative strength of gravitational forces.

answerOptions.text: Telescopes are necessary to see all planets.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states the four planets were invisible 'without the aid of a glass' (telescope), but it does not claim that all planets require a telescope, nor is this the main conclusion about the former belief.

answerOptions.text: There are more than four planets in the solar system.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage mentions observing four planets (moons) around Jupiter, but the primary conclusion relates to what this observation reveals about the center of revolution for heavenly bodies, not merely the total count of planets.

questions.passage:
The Starry Messenger (Excerpt)

Galileo Galilei, 1610

Through my telescope I have observed four planets revolving about Jupiter, invisible without the aid of a glass. These observations prove that not all heavenly bodies revolve around the Earth, as was formerly believed.

questions.questionText: The author's account primarily illustrates which aspect of scientific advancement?

answerOptions.text: The importance of new technologies in challenging established theories.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage highlights how observations made 'Through my telescope' led to new evidence that disproved a 'formerly believed' idea, directly linking new technology (the telescope) to challenging existing scientific understanding.

answerOptions.text: The difficulty scientists face in convincing others of new discoveries.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage describes the observation and conclusion, but it does not detail any challenges the author faced in gaining acceptance for the new idea.

answerOptions.text: The need for multiple scientists to confirm a single observation.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage presents the author's personal observations and conclusions, without mentioning collaboration or confirmation by other scientists.

answerOptions.text: The constant discovery of entirely new celestial bodies.
answerOptions.rationale: While new celestial bodies were observed, the core point is how their revolution around Jupiter disproved the geocentric model, not just the mere discovery of new objects. The impact on the existing scientific model is key.

questions.passage:
The Starry Messenger (Excerpt)

Galileo Galilei, 1610

Through my telescope I have observed four planets revolving about Jupiter, invisible without the aid of a glass. These observations prove that not all heavenly bodies revolve around the Earth, as was formerly believed.

questions.questionText: Which energy source is projected to have the highest total consumption in 2040 on the graph?

answerOptions.text: Petroleum
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The petroleum line remains above the other energy-source lines at the end of the graph.

answerOptions.text: Nuclear
answerOptions.rationale: Nuclear is much lower than petroleum on the graph.

answerOptions.text: Other energy
answerOptions.rationale: Other energy does not reach the highest level shown.

answerOptions.text: None of the sources increase by 2040
answerOptions.rationale: Several lines increase across the time period shown.

stimulusImage.alt: Line graph showing world energy consumption by source (petroleum, coal, natural gas, nuclear, other) from 2010 to 2040.

questions.questionText: What is the main advantage of showing all five energy sources on the same graph?

answerOptions.text: It allows direct comparison of long-term trends among different energy sources.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. A shared graph makes it easier to compare relative growth and overall levels across sources.

answerOptions.text: It guarantees every energy source will grow at the same rate.
answerOptions.rationale: The graph compares trends; it does not force them to be equal.

answerOptions.text: It shows the exact location of each power plant in the world.
answerOptions.rationale: This is a consumption graph, not a geographic map.

answerOptions.text: It measures atmospheric humidity instead of energy use.
answerOptions.rationale: The graph is explicitly about world energy consumption.

stimulusImage.alt: Line graph showing world energy consumption by source (petroleum, coal, natural gas, nuclear, other) from 2010 to 2040.

questions.questionText: Which drop provides the best approximation of free fall near Earth’s surface?

answerOptions.text: Drop A (smooth metal sphere)
answerOptions.rationale: The streamlined sphere experiences minimal air resistance.

answerOptions.text: Drop B (sphere with parachute)
answerOptions.rationale: The parachute dramatically increases drag, breaking free-fall conditions.

answerOptions.text: Drop C (crumpled paper ball)
answerOptions.rationale: Paper is light and affected by drag, so it deviates from free fall.

answerOptions.text: None of the drops model free fall because gravity varies.
answerOptions.rationale: Gravity is effectively constant over the small height.

questions.passage: A physics class compares motion sensor data for objects dropped from a balcony. Drop A uses a smooth metal sphere, Drop B uses the same sphere with a parachute, and Drop C uses a crumpled paper ball. Students graph velocity vs. time for each drop.

questions.questionText: Why does the velocity-time graph for Drop B level off quickly?

answerOptions.text: The sensor lost connection mid-trial.
answerOptions.rationale: Data show consistent readings, so this is unlikely.

answerOptions.text: The parachute adds drag until net force becomes zero.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Terminal velocity occurs when drag equals weight.

answerOptions.text: The mass of the object increases during the fall.
answerOptions.rationale: Mass stays constant; drag is the changing factor.

answerOptions.text: Gravity weakens at higher speeds.
answerOptions.rationale: Gravity is independent of speed at these scales.

questions.passage: A physics class compares motion sensor data for objects dropped from a balcony. Drop A uses a smooth metal sphere, Drop B uses the same sphere with a parachute, and Drop C uses a crumpled paper ball. Students graph velocity vs. time for each drop.
